Also the default being `~/.powerline-shell.json` is not quite correct. There are two issues:

- The preferred default is actually the `os.path.join(os.environ.get("XDG_CONFIG_HOME", "~/.config"), "powerline-shell", "config.json")` file - ~/.powerline-shell.json is going to be removed eventually.
- I think it would be better to remove `default` entirely from this option. If someone were to do `powerline-shell --help` it would say the default location is `~/.powerline-shell.json`, but if they happened to have a file called `powerline-shell.json` in their current directory, then the default value would not actually be used. This confusing behavior.

I think there is an error here. Correct me if I am wrong. The original code you had was:
```
special.gamma((p * x) + q) / (special.gamma(p * x) * special.gamma(q))
```

taking the log of this:

```
np.log(special.gamma((p * x) + q)) - np.log(special.gamma(p * x)) - np.log(special.gamma(q))
```

but you have separated the third term into `np.log(special.gamma(p)) - np.log(special.gamma(x))`.

There are others as well, such as blog posts, which we apparently don't have a resource for in this library. Here is the full list of resources and subresources with a `/count` endpoint, according to [the documentation](https://developer.bigcommerce.com/api)

* BlogPost
* Redirect
* Customer
* CustomerGroup
* Country
* State
* Coupon
* Order
* OrderProduct
* Shipment
* Brand
* BulkPricingRule
* Category
* ConfigurableField
* CustomField
* Option
* OptionSet
* Product
* ProductImage
* ProductRule
* ProductVideo
* ProductSKU

Unfortunately, not all of these are available in this library. However, I think it makes sense to add `CountableApiResource` to those that do exist. To do so, we'll probably need to create a `CountableApiSubResource` as well.

Well, order cannot matter in javac (the plugin options go into an unordered map, so the order is lost almost immediately). They could hypothetically matter in scalac (the plugins are loaded in cmd-line order, as far as I can tell), but they don't in practice - the loading order doesn't affect when each plugin runs - that happens at whatever phase the plugin asked to be run in.

So now it's down to whether we want to err on the side of hypothetical caution, at the expense of triggering spurious invalidation. I just try to avoid spurious invalidation whenever possible. 

We can always change this in the future, should we discover that order matters. That would be a backwards-compatible change, while the reverse would not.

Ok, I was thinking about this too, and some clarification: discovery protocol implemented by "xiaomi_gw.py" is **not** a discovery protocol for "Xiaomi products", because majority of "Xiaomi products" are made by varying "subsidiaries", which have little in common on technical side (except for integration into Xiaomi's cloud). In particular, the protocol used by "xiaomi_gw.py" is currently known to be used only by Xiaomi Mi Home Gateway. That's why the protocol module is called "xiaomi_gw.py" (discoverable module is called "xiaomi.py", because it should match HA's component (or service?) name, and unfortunately, HA uses "xiaomi" for the *Gateway* name, which will lead to confusion as more wide-varying "Xiaomi" devices are added to HA).

It's possible that this protocol will be used in other "Xiaomi" products, but it will never be the only protocol to discover all "Xiaomi" devices. Hence, I err on the side of specificity, to avoid even remotely the situation that somebody's Xiaomi Water Tap (for example) will be recognized as Home Gateway.

@balloob, you suggest to err on the side of generality. I usually love that, but not this time, due to the reasons above. However, seeing your point, I may imagine following change:

1. xiaomi_gw.py gets renamed to xiaomi_whoami.py, with docstrings that this module implements Xiaomi UDP, JSON based "whoami" protocol, which is currently known to be used by just Gateway.
2. xiaomi.py discoverable filters just "gateway" entries, as this discoverable indeed represent only gateway.

Does that sound good?

There are going to be some issues with this potentially.

Currently, we register custom serializers/deserializers in the serialization context for non-primitive objects. This is done under the hood, e.g., 

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/8e687cbc9838fce0b1d8190c7851b7d573562487/python/ray/worker.py#L305-L339

Basically, a worker tries to serialize an object that it doesn't know how to serialize. Pyarrow raises an exception. We attempt to define a custom serializer on the fly. We broadcast that custom serializer to all the other workers/drivers so they know how to serialize that object also.

When we transfer objects from the gateway to the client, we probably want to use `ray.cloudpickle`. However, the gateway may need to also subscribe to the relevant Redis channel in order to get the custom functions needed to deserialize custom objects.

It'd be ok to not support this in a first pass, but we should make sure that we understand the issue and that we're catching the error as anticipated.
